one_line_takeaway: Adding a co-evolving three-class evaluator role to attacker-defender self-play RL prevents red-team pattern collapse (diversity score 0.588 vs 0.004 without it) and lets the defender gain large safety improvements (e.g., ASR on JailBreakBench 31.5% to 4.6%) without measurable loss of general reasoning ability.
key_finding: After 16 training iterations (3200 steps), MRed-14B's ASR increased from 13.0% to 32.0% against DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B, from 21.84% to 67.75% against Qwen3-8B, and reached 90% against Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1 (up from ~60%). MBlue-14B's ASR on AIR-Bench 2024 dropped from 13.9% to 4.4% and on JailBreakBench from 31.5% to 4.6% over 10 iterations, while general-reasoning benchmarks (LiveCodeBench, GPQA, AIME25, IFEval) showed no significant degradation and slight gains in nearly half the comparisons (e.g., MBlue-14B GPQA 64.93 vs Qwen3-14B baseline 64.33; MBlue-8B LiveCodeBench 51.00 vs Qwen3-8B baseline 49.71). MEval-14B's three-class classification accuracy rose from 97.0% to 98.2% over 10 iterations (MEval-4B: 48.2% to 56.2%; MEval-8B: 54.9% to 64.3%). Ablations show the full method (with closed-loop training + diversity penalty) achieves the highest output-diversity score OD=0.588 versus 0.156 with the closed loop removed and only diversity penalty retained, versus 0.514 with the penalty removed and closed loop retained, versus 0.004 for neither; the no-loop/no-penalty variant collapses in training entropy and, despite reaching the highest ASR against the weaker Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B (98.2%), generalizes poorly, only attaining 2.8% ASR against Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, versus 6.6% for the full method -- illustrating that pattern-collapsed attackers overfit to a single target and lose transferability.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TriPlay-RL, a closed-loop, three-role (attacker/defender/evaluator) GRPO-based reinforcement-learning framework for LLM safety alignment, extending prior two-role (attacker-defender) self-play by adding an evolving evaluator (MEval) whose fine-grained three-class judgments (unsafe/refusal/helpful-guidance) drive rewards for both the attacker (MRed) and defender (MBlue). All three roles are alternately updated in sequential phases (PRed -> PBlue -> PEval) using GRPO/RLVR, starting from Qwen3-4B/8B/14B and only 200 seed HarmBench prompts wrapped via nine templates into 1,800 seed adversarial prompts, requiring near-zero manual annotation.
threat_model: The 'attacker' MRed is trained to generate adversarial (jailbreak-style) prompts that wrap benign-seeming framings (e.g., historian-of-chemistry framing) around harmful base requests drawn from HarmBench, with the explicit goal of eliciting unsafe content from target defense LLMs (self-trained MBlue as well as external targets: DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-8B, Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1, Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, GLM-4.6). This is a controlled, research-only red-teaming setup: the authors explicitly state they do not release attack prompts or trained attacker models to prevent real-world misuse (Ethical Considerations section), and the attacker's purpose within the paper is to serve as a training signal to improve the defender, not to be deployed as a standalone jailbreak tool.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) MRed, MBlue, and MEval are all initialized from the same base model family (Qwen3) and instantiated as three separate model instances rather than heterogeneous or shared models -- unexplored whether heterogeneous capability models or a single shared model instance across roles would work; (2) the paper does not analyze the game-theoretic properties of the three-way interaction (no Nash equilibrium or Pareto-frontier analysis) nor provide mechanisms to monitor/control relative growth rates across roles, risking destabilized training or suboptimal equilibria if one role improves too fast/slow; (3) no exploration of incorporating external SFT data (safety or adversarial datasets) before/during RL training; (4) the paper explicitly notes an unaddressed fundamental trade-off: optimizing MRed for attack likely degrades its own safety behavior, while strengthening MBlue may suppress MRed's adversarial effectiveness -- this tension is not resolved. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the text): MEval's accuracy for the 4B and 8B variants (56.2% and 64.3% after training) remains well below the 14B variant's 98.2%, meaning reward-signal quality is highly size-dependent, and MEval training data quality itself relies on external judge models (Octopus, Llama-Guard, gpt-oss) whose own reliability is not independently validated in this paper. Attack-success judging for MRed's headline ASR numbers relies on a single external judge (GPT-5.2), introducing judge dependency not cross-validated against a second judge.

datasets_used: HarmBench (200 seed prompts, 9 prompt-wrapping templates -> 1,800 seed adversarial prompts)
quantitative_results: MRed-14B ASR vs DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B: 13.0% -> 32.0% over 16 iterations
MRed-14B ASR vs Qwen3-8B: 21.84% -> 67.75% over 16 iterations
MRed-14B ASR vs Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1: ~60% -> 90% over 16 iterations
MBlue-14B ASR on AIR-Bench 2024: 13.9% -> 4.4% over 10 iterations
MBlue-14B ASR on JailBreakBench: 31.5% -> 4.6% over 10 iterations

General reasoning retention (avg@32): MBlue-14B GPQA 64.93 vs Qwen3-14B baseline 64.33; MBlue-8B LiveCodeBench 51.00 vs Qwen3-8B baseline 49.71; MBlue-4B AIME25 65.21 vs Qwen3-4B baseline 67.40 (slight drop)
Ablation output-diversity (OD) score: w/L+w/D = 0.588, w/o-loop+w/D = 0.156, w/L+w/o-D = 0.514, w/o-loop+w/o-D = 0.004
Ablation ASR vs Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B / Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: w/L+w/D = 85.4%/6.6%; w/o-loop+w/D = 95.1%/2.8%; w/L+w/o-D = 90.9%/6.0%; w/o-loop+w/o-D = 98.2%/0.2%
Multi- vs single-defense-model ablation ASR (4th iteration) vs DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B/Qwen3-8B/Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1: multiple = 29.1%/64.1%/85.4%, single = 22.8%/60.1%/77.6%
